1:
"Do we begin again to commend ourselves? or need we, as some others, epistles of commendation to you, or letters of commendation from you?" The witnesses note Paul's disclaiming need of letters of commendation. On "do we begin again to commend ourselves?" the grammatical voices note the question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul anticipates the charge that he is praising himself: does he need, like others, to commend himself anew? — he asks ironically, knowing the Corinthians know him.

On "epistles of commendation," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Chrysostom note that "some others" (the false teachers) came with letters of commendation, to or from the churches; Paul needs no such credentials with the Corinthians, whom he had himself converted.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the point: Paul's true commendation is not paper credentials but the Corinthians themselves, his converts — the living proof of his apostleship. The verse displays Paul's disclaiming need of letters of commendation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates the charge of self-praise — does he need, like others, to commend himself anew? — asking ironically, knowing they know him (Barnes, Robertson); while "some others" (the false teachers) came with letters of commendation, Paul needs no such credentials with the Corinthians, his own converts (Barnes, Chrysostom). Chrysostom and Calvin note his true commendation is the Corinthians themselves. The verse displays the disclaiming of letters. The verse marks the question: commend ourselves? The verse displays the contrast: with the false teachers. The verse displays the credentials: not needed. The verse displays the proof: his converts. The disclaiming displays Paul's commendation in his converts, not paper. The verse displays the irony: self-praise? The verse displays the letters: others' credentials. The verse displays the converts: his proof. Paul's disclaiming need of letters of commendation displays the ground of his apostolic standing: unlike the false teachers who came with paper credentials, he needs none with the Corinthians, his own converts — they are his living commendation. The verse displays the apostle's true credentials, which he names directly (vv.2-3). The whole question carries a quiet irony, for the very need to ask it had been forced upon Paul by rivals who trafficked in such documents, and he turns their boast on its head by pointing to a credential no parchment could supply.
2:
"Ye are our epistle written in our hearts, known and read of all men:" The brief verse notes the Corinthians as Paul's living letter of commendation. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul declares the Corinthians themselves are "our epistle" (our letter of recommendation) — the proof of his ministry, "written in our hearts" (cherished there with affection), and "known and read of all men" (open to all as evidence of his work).

On "written in our hearts," the witnesses note the affection. Barnes notes the Corinthians are inscribed on Paul's heart — he carries them with love; their conversion is his dearest credential.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: the changed lives of the Corinthians are a letter that all can read, testifying to the genuineness and power of Paul's apostolic ministry. The verse displays the Corinthians as Paul's living letter.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Corinthians themselves are "our epistle" — the proof of Paul's ministry, "written in our hearts" (cherished with affection) and "known and read of all men" (open to all as evidence, Barnes, Robertson); inscribed on Paul's heart, carried with love, his dearest credential (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note their changed lives are a letter all can read, testifying to his ministry's genuineness. The verse displays the Corinthians as Paul's letter. The verse marks the figure: our epistle. The verse displays the place: in our hearts. The verse displays the openness: read of all men. The verse displays the testimony: to his ministry. The figure displays the converts as a letter all can read. The verse displays the epistle: the Corinthians. The verse displays the affection: in his heart. The verse displays the witness: to all men. The Corinthians as Paul's living letter of commendation display the proof of his ministry: their changed lives, cherished in his heart and open to all, are a letter that every one can read, testifying to the genuineness and power of his apostolic work. The verse displays the converts as Paul's credential, written by the Spirit (v.3).
3:
"Forasmuch as ye are manifestly declared to be the epistle of Christ ministered by us, written not with ink, but with the Spirit of the living God; not in tables of stone, but in fleshy tables of the heart." The witnesses note the Corinthians as the epistle of Christ written by the Spirit. On "the epistle of Christ ministered by us," the grammatical voices note the authorship. Barnes and Robertson note that the Corinthians are "the epistle of Christ" — Christ is the true author of the letter (their conversion), Paul only the minister (the instrument, the pen) by whom it was written.

On "written not with ink, but with the Spirit of the living God," the witnesses note the writing. Barnes and Calvin note the letter was written not with ink (as human letters) but by "the Spirit of the living God" — the Holy Spirit inscribed it, by his regenerating work in their hearts.

On "not in tables of stone, but in fleshy tables of the heart," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the contrast with the law: unlike the law written on stone tables (Exodus), this new-covenant writing is on "fleshy tables of the heart" (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26) — inward, living, transforming. The verse displays the Corinthians as the epistle of Christ written by the Spirit.

The witnesses agree: the Corinthians are "the epistle of Christ" — Christ the true author of their conversion, Paul only the minister by whom it was written (Barnes, Robertson); written not with ink but by "the Spirit of the living God," who inscribed it by his regenerating work (Barnes, Calvin); not on stone tables (as the law) but on "fleshy tables of the heart" (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26), inward and transforming (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays the epistle of Christ. The verse marks the author: Christ. The verse displays the writing: by the Spirit. The verse displays the contrast: stone vs the heart. The verse displays the newness: the new covenant. The epistle displays the Spirit's inward writing in the new covenant. The verse displays the author: Christ. The verse displays the pen: the Spirit. The verse displays the tablet: the heart. The Corinthians as the epistle of Christ written by the Spirit display the inward, transforming work of the new covenant: Christ, through Paul's ministry, wrote upon their hearts not with ink but by the Spirit of the living God — not on stone tables as the law, but on the living tablets of the heart. The verse displays the new-covenant writing of the Spirit, grounding Paul's confidence (vv.4-5).
4:
"And such trust have we through Christ to God-ward:" The brief verse notes Paul's confidence grounded through Christ toward God. The grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul affirms that he has "such trust" (confidence, assurance) — concerning his ministry and its fruit (the Corinthians) — and this "through Christ" and "to God-ward" (directed toward God).

On "through Christ to God-ward," the witnesses note the source and direction. Barnes notes Paul's confidence is not self-derived but comes through Christ, and is exercised before God — a humble, God-referred assurance, not boasting.

The Reformed Calvin notes the guard: Paul, having spoken of the glory of his ministry, immediately ascribes his confidence to Christ and refers it to God, lest he seem to glory in himself. The verse displays Paul's confidence grounded through Christ toward God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul affirms he has "such trust" (confidence) concerning his ministry and its fruit, and this "through Christ" and "to God-ward" (directed toward God, Barnes, Robertson); his confidence not self-derived but coming through Christ and exercised before God, a humble God-referred assurance (Barnes). Calvin notes Paul ascribes his confidence to Christ and refers it to God, lest he seem to glory in himself. The verse displays Paul's confidence. The verse marks the trust: such confidence. The verse displays the source: through Christ. The verse displays the direction: to God-ward. The verse displays the humility: not self-derived. The confidence displays Paul's assurance referred to Christ and God. The verse displays the trust: in his ministry. The verse displays the channel: Christ. The verse displays the reference: God. Paul's confidence grounded through Christ toward God displays the humble source of his assurance: the trust he has concerning his ministry is not from himself but through Christ, and is exercised toward God — a confidence that glories not in self but in the Lord. The verse displays the God-referred ground of Paul's assurance, disclaimed of self-sufficiency (v.5). Such an assurance, resting wholly on Christ and presented before the face of God, is the very opposite of the self-vaunting Paul disclaims, for it boasts only in what God has wrought and dares to claim nothing as springing from the minister himself.
5:
"Not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think any thing as of ourselves; but our sufficiency is of God;" The witnesses note that all sufficiency is of God, not of ourselves. On "not that we are sufficient of ourselves," the grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul explicitly denies any self-sufficiency: he is not adequate "of himself" even "to think any thing as of ourselves" — not even his thoughts, much less his ministry, originate in his own power.

On "our sufficiency is of God," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Calvin note that all his adequacy for the work comes "of God" — God alone makes him fit; every right thought and every fruit of his labour is from God.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the doctrine: this is a strong testimony to human insufficiency and the necessity of grace — not even a good thought is of ourselves; all is of God, who gives both the will and the power. The verse displays that all sufficiency is of God, not of ourselves.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul denies any self-sufficiency — he is not adequate "of himself" even "to think any thing as of ourselves," not even his thoughts originating in his own power (Barnes, Robertson); all his adequacy coming "of God," who alone makes him fit (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note this strong testimony to human insufficiency and the necessity of grace — not even a good thought is of ourselves, all being of God. The verse displays sufficiency of God. The verse marks the disclaimer: not of ourselves. The verse displays the depth: not even to think. The verse displays the source: of God. The verse displays the doctrine: human insufficiency. The sufficiency displays all adequacy traced to God's grace. The verse displays the insufficiency: of self. The verse displays the thinking: not our own. The verse displays the source: God. That all sufficiency is of God, not of ourselves, displays the depth of human dependence on grace: Paul is not adequate even to think a good thought of himself, much less to do the work of the ministry — his whole sufficiency is from God. The verse displays the radical disclaimer of self and the ascription of all to God, who made him a minister of the new covenant (v.6).
6:
"Who also hath made us able ministers of the new testament; not of the letter, but of the spirit: for the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life." The witnesses note the ministry of the new covenant and the letter that kills — the great crux. On "able ministers of the new testament," the grammatical voices note the office. Barnes and Robertson note God has made Paul "sufficient" (not merely able) as a minister of the "new covenant" (the gospel, in contrast to the old covenant of Moses).

On "not of the letter, but of the spirit," and "the letter killeth, the spirit giveth life," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and the Reformed Calvin hold "letter" means the law / the old written covenant (the law's bare commandment, which condemns and so "kills"), and "spirit" the gospel / new covenant ministered by the Spirit (which gives life); they explicitly reject Origen's allegorical reading (letter = the literal sense, spirit = the allegorical/spiritual meaning) as a "spurious signification" that bred pernicious error. Wesleyan-arminian Clarke offers a middle: "letter" is resting in the bare external letter (of either covenant) without its spiritual meaning, "spirit" the inward meaning that leads to Christ.

The witnesses note the core: the written law condemns and kills, while the Spirit of the gospel gives spiritual and eternal life. The verse displays the ministry of the new covenant and the letter that kills, with the great crux.

The witnesses agree the core: the written law, of itself, condemns and so "kills," while the Spirit of the gospel "gives life" (Barnes, Calvin, Henry, Wesley). But the meaning of "letter" and "spirit" divides them: Barnes, Ellicott, and Calvin take "letter" as the law / the old written covenant (the bare commandment that condemns) and "spirit" as the gospel / new covenant ministered by the Spirit — explicitly rejecting Origen's allegorical reading (letter = literal sense, spirit = allegorical meaning) as a spurious error; Clarke takes "letter" as resting in the bare external letter without its spiritual meaning, "spirit" as the inward sense that leads to Christ. The verse displays the new covenant and the letter that kills. The verse marks the office: minister of the new covenant. The verse displays the contrast: letter and spirit. The verse displays the debate: law/gospel or literal/allegorical. The verse displays the effect: kills vs gives life. The crux displays the letter condemning, the Spirit giving life. The verse displays the letter: killing. The verse displays the spirit: life-giving. The verse displays the covenant: new. The ministry of the new covenant and the letter that kills display the great contrast Paul draws: the written law, of itself, condemns and kills, while the Spirit of the gospel gives life — "letter" read by most as the law / old covenant (against Origen's literal-allegorical scheme) and the "spirit" as the life-giving gospel ministered by the Spirit. The verse displays the superiority of the Spirit's ministry, developed in the comparison of glories (vv.7-11).
7:
"But if the ministration of death, written and engraven in stones, was glorious, so that the children of Israel could not stedfastly behold the face of Moses for the glory of his countenance; which glory was to be done away:" The witnesses note the glory of the ministration of death, yet passing. On "the ministration of death, written and engraven in stones," the grammatical voices note the old covenant. Barnes and Robertson note Paul calls the law (the ten commandments, "written and engraven in stones," Exodus 31:18) "the ministration of death" — because, condemning sin, it brought death, not life.

On "the children of Israel could not stedfastly behold the face of Moses," the witnesses note its glory. Barnes and Chrysostom note that even this ministration had a real "glory" — so that Moses's face shone after Sinai (Exodus 34:29-30), and Israel could not gaze steadily upon it.

On "which glory was to be done away," the Reformed Calvin notes the transience: that glory was fading, temporary, destined to pass away — a mark of the old covenant's provisional character. The verse displays the glory of the ministration of death, yet passing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul calls the law "the ministration of death," written and engraven in stones (Exodus 31:18), because, condemning sin, it brought death (Barnes, Robertson); yet even it had a real glory — Moses's face shone so that Israel could not gaze steadily upon it (Exodus 34:29-30, Barnes, Chrysostom); but that glory "was to be done away," fading and temporary, a mark of the old covenant's provisional character (Calvin). The verse displays the glory of the ministration of death. The verse marks the covenant: the ministration of death. The verse displays the glory: Moses's shining face. The verse displays the transience: glory done away. The verse displays the character: provisional. The glory displays the old covenant's real but fading splendour. The verse displays the law: written in stone. The verse displays the shining: of Moses. The verse displays the fading: of the glory. The glory of the ministration of death, yet passing, displays the real but provisional splendour of the old covenant: the law, written in stone and bringing death, had so true a glory that Moses's face shone and Israel could not look steadily on it — yet that glory was fading, destined to pass away. The verse displays the transient glory of the law, set against the abiding glory of the Spirit (vv.8-11).
8:
"How shall not the ministration of the spirit be rather glorious?" The brief verse notes the surpassing glory of the ministration of the Spirit. The grammatical voices note the argument. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's a fortiori: if the ministration of death (the law) had glory, "how shall not the ministration of the spirit (the gospel) be rather (much more) glorious?" — the greater glory belongs to the new covenant.

On "the ministration of the spirit," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes notes the gospel is "the ministration of the Spirit" — the dispensation through which the Holy Spirit is given and life imparted — over against the law, which gave death; its glory must therefore far exceed.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the inference: since the gospel gives life by the Spirit, while the law gave death, its glory is incomparably greater. The verse displays the surpassing glory of the ministration of the Spirit.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's a fortiori — if the ministration of death (the law) had glory, how shall not the ministration of the spirit (the gospel) be much more glorious? (Barnes, Robertson); the gospel being "the ministration of the Spirit," through which the Holy Spirit is given and life imparted, over against the law which gave death (Barnes). Wesley notes that since the gospel gives life by the Spirit while the law gave death, its glory is incomparably greater. The verse displays the surpassing glory. The verse marks the argument: how much more. The verse displays the contrast: Spirit vs death. The verse displays the gift: the Spirit given. The verse displays the conclusion: greater glory. The argument displays the gospel's surpassing glory. The verse displays the ministration: of the Spirit. The verse displays the glory: greater. The verse displays the life: imparted. The surpassing glory of the ministration of the Spirit displays the a fortiori force of Paul's argument: if even the law that gave death had glory, much more must the gospel — the ministration of the life-giving Spirit — be glorious. The verse displays the greater glory of the new covenant, pressed in the further contrast (vv.9-11).
9:
"For if the ministration of condemnation be glory, much more doth the ministration of righteousness exceed in glory." The brief verse notes that the ministration of righteousness exceeds the ministration of condemnation in glory. The grammatical voices note the parallel. Barnes and Robertson note Paul restates the contrast: the law is "the ministration of condemnation" (it condemns the sinner), the gospel "the ministration of righteousness" (it reveals and bestows the righteousness of God by faith); if the former had glory, the latter "much more exceeds."

On "the ministration of righteousness," the witnesses note the gospel's excellence. Barnes and Henry note the gospel imparts righteousness — justification, the gift of God's righteousness through Christ — and so far surpasses the condemning law in glory.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the contrast: condemnation belongs to the law, righteousness to the gospel; the dispensation that justifies must be more glorious than the one that condemns. The verse displays that the ministration of righteousness exceeds in glory.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul restates the contrast — the law is "the ministration of condemnation" (it condemns the sinner), the gospel "the ministration of righteousness" (it reveals and bestows God's righteousness by faith); if the former had glory, the latter much more exceeds (Barnes, Robertson); the gospel imparting justification through Christ, far surpassing the condemning law (Barnes, Henry). Wesley notes the dispensation that justifies must be more glorious than the one that condemns. The verse displays the ministration of righteousness. The verse marks the law: condemnation. The verse displays the gospel: righteousness. The verse displays the comparison: much more glory. The verse displays the gift: justification. The contrast displays the gospel of righteousness surpassing the law of condemnation. The verse displays the condemnation: of the law. The verse displays the righteousness: of the gospel. The verse displays the excess: of glory. That the ministration of righteousness exceeds the ministration of condemnation in glory displays the gospel's surpassing excellence: where the law condemns the sinner, the gospel bestows the righteousness of God by faith — and the dispensation that justifies far exceeds in glory the one that condemns. The verse displays the greater glory of the justifying gospel, sealed by the eclipsing of the old (vv.10-11).
10:
"For even that which was made glorious had no glory in this respect, by reason of the glory that excelleth." The brief verse notes the glory of the law eclipsed by the surpassing glory of the gospel. The grammatical voices note the eclipse. Barnes and Robertson note Paul presses the point: that which "was made glorious" (the law, with its real glory) now "had no glory in this respect" — its glory is so eclipsed by the surpassing glory of the gospel that, by comparison, it has none.

On "the glory that excelleth," the witnesses note the comparison. Barnes and Henry note the figure (as elsewhere): as the stars are unseen when the sun rises, so the glory of the law is lost in the far greater glory of the gospel; not that the law had no glory in itself, but that it pales beside the new covenant.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: the comparison is relative — the law's glory was real, but so far surpassed by the gospel's that it seems as nothing in its presence. The verse displays the glory of the law eclipsed by the gospel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: that which "was made glorious" (the law) now "had no glory in this respect" — its glory so eclipsed by the surpassing glory of the gospel that by comparison it has none (Barnes, Robertson); as the stars are unseen when the sun rises, so the law's glory is lost in the gospel's, not absent in itself but paling beside the new covenant (Barnes, Henry). Calvin notes the comparison is relative — the law's real glory seems as nothing beside the gospel's. The verse displays the law's glory eclipsed. The verse marks the eclipse: no glory by comparison. The verse displays the figure: stars and sun. The verse displays the relativity: real yet surpassed. The verse displays the surpassing: the gospel's glory. The eclipse displays the law's glory swallowed by the gospel's. The verse displays the law: once glorious. The verse displays the gospel: excelling. The verse displays the comparison: relative. The glory of the law eclipsed by the surpassing glory of the gospel displays the relative comparison Paul presses: the law's real glory is so far exceeded by the gospel's that, like stars before the rising sun, it seems to have no glory at all beside it. The verse displays the surpassing brightness of the new covenant, confirmed by the law's passing (v.11).
11:
"For if that which is done away was glorious, much more that which remaineth is glorious." The brief verse notes the contrast between the passing law and the abiding gospel. The grammatical voices note the final comparison. Barnes and Robertson note Paul concludes the comparison: "that which is done away" (the law, the temporary dispensation, now abolished) was glorious; "much more" must "that which remaineth" (the gospel, the permanent, abiding covenant) be glorious.

On "that which remaineth," the witnesses note the permanence. Barnes notes the gospel is the dispensation that "remains" — it will not pass away or be superseded, but endures to the end; its abiding nature adds to its glory.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the climax: the transitory had glory; the permanent must have far more; the gospel's perpetuity crowns its surpassing glory. The verse displays the contrast between the passing law and the abiding gospel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes — "that which is done away" (the law, now abolished) was glorious; much more must "that which remaineth" (the gospel, the abiding covenant) be glorious (Barnes, Robertson); the gospel being the dispensation that "remains," enduring to the end, its abiding nature adding to its glory (Barnes). Wesley notes the transitory had glory, the permanent must have far more. The verse displays the passing law and abiding gospel. The verse marks the law: done away. The verse displays the gospel: remaineth. The verse displays the contrast: transitory and permanent. The verse displays the climax: much more glory. The contrast displays the abiding gospel surpassing the passing law. The verse displays the abolished: the law. The verse displays the abiding: the gospel. The verse displays the glory: crowned by permanence. The contrast between the passing law and the abiding gospel displays the climax of Paul's argument: if the temporary, now-abolished law had glory, the permanent, abiding gospel must have far more — its perpetuity crowning its surpassing glory. The verse displays the enduring glory of the new covenant, which gives Paul his boldness (vv.12-13). The very word he chooses, set in the present tense, marks the gospel as a covenant that does not merely outlast the law but abides without end, so that its permanence is itself a further token of the surpassing glory he has been pressing throughout the comparison.
12:
"Seeing then that we have such hope, we use great plainness of speech:" The brief verse notes Paul's boldness and plainness grounded in his hope. The grammatical voices note the confidence. Barnes and Henry note that "seeing we have such hope" — such a glorious, abiding ministry and its hope — Paul and the apostles "use great plainness (boldness, openness) of speech," preaching the gospel clearly and confidently, without concealment.

On "plainness of speech," the witnesses note the openness. Barnes notes the word means boldness, freedom, openness; unlike the veiled obscurity of the old covenant, the apostles speak the gospel plainly and unreservedly.

The Reformed Calvin notes the contrast (anticipating v.13): the clarity of the gospel ministry stands over against the veiled, obscure ministry of Moses; the glory of the new covenant warrants and produces this boldness. The verse displays Paul's boldness and plainness grounded in his hope.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "seeing we have such hope" — such a glorious, abiding ministry — Paul and the apostles "use great plainness (boldness, openness) of speech," preaching the gospel clearly and confidently without concealment (Barnes, Henry); the word meaning boldness and freedom, unlike the veiled obscurity of the old covenant (Barnes). Calvin notes the clarity of the gospel stands over against the veiled ministry of Moses. The verse displays Paul's boldness. The verse marks the ground: such hope. The verse displays the manner: plainness of speech. The verse displays the openness: bold and clear. The verse displays the contrast: with the veiled old covenant. The boldness displays the clear, open preaching of the gospel. The verse displays the hope: glorious. The verse displays the plainness: of speech. The verse displays the confidence: unconcealed. Paul's boldness and plainness grounded in his hope display the confidence the glorious gospel produces: because the new covenant is so glorious and abiding, the apostles preach it with great openness and clarity — unlike the veiled obscurity of the old. The verse displays the apostolic boldness, contrasted with Moses's veil (vv.13-16). The plainness he claims is therefore no mere matter of temperament but the native fruit of the new covenant itself, whose unveiled glory frees its ministers to speak without disguise, concealment, or the studied obscurity that belonged to the order now passing away.
13:
"And not as Moses, which put a vail over his face, that the children of Israel could not stedfastly look to the end of that which is abolished:" The witnesses note the veil of Moses, hiding the fading glory. On "Moses, which put a vail over his face," the grammatical voices note the historical fact. Barnes and Robertson note Paul refers to Moses veiling his face (Exodus 34:33-35) after speaking with God, because his face shone — but here Paul gives it a symbolic turn.

On "that the children of Israel could not stedfastly look to the end of that which is abolished," the witnesses note the symbolism. Barnes and Calvin note the veil prevented Israel from gazing on the "end" (the termination, the fading) of "that which is abolished" (the transient glory of the law) — the veil symbolizing the obscurity of the old covenant, which hid from Israel that its glory was passing and pointed beyond itself to Christ.

The Reformed Calvin notes the contrast: Moses's veiled ministry, obscuring the fading glory, stands over against the apostles' open, plain preaching; the old covenant was deliberately obscure, the new is clear. The verse displays the veil of Moses, hiding the fading glory.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul refers to Moses veiling his shining face (Exodus 34:33-35), giving it a symbolic turn (Barnes, Robertson); the veil prevented Israel from gazing on the "end" (the fading) of the transient glory of the abolished law, symbolizing the obscurity of the old covenant that hid from Israel its passing nature and its pointing to Christ (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes Moses's veiled ministry stands over against the apostles' open preaching. The verse displays the veil of Moses. The verse marks the fact: Moses veiled his face. The verse displays the symbolism: hiding the fading glory. The verse displays the obscurity: of the old covenant. The verse displays the contrast: with apostolic plainness. The veil displays the old covenant's obscurity hiding its transience. The verse displays the veil: over Moses's face. The verse displays the fading: of the glory. The verse displays the obscurity: of the law. The veil of Moses, hiding the fading glory, displays the obscurity of the old covenant: as Moses veiled his shining face so Israel could not see its glory fade, so the old covenant was deliberately obscure, hiding from Israel that its glory was passing and pointing beyond itself to Christ — over against the apostles' open, plain preaching. The verse displays the symbolic veil of the law, applied to Israel's continuing blindness (vv.14-15).
14:
"But their minds were blinded: for until this day remaineth the same vail untaken away in the reading of the old testament; which vail is done away in Christ." The witnesses note Israel's blindness and the veil removed only in Christ. On "their minds were blinded," the grammatical voices note the hardening. Barnes and Robertson note Israel's "minds were blinded" (hardened, dulled) — they did not perceive the true meaning and end of the law; a judicial and self-incurred blindness.

On "until this day remaineth the same vail... in the reading of the old testament," the witnesses note the continuing veil. Barnes notes that the veil remains "to this day" — when the Jews read the old covenant (the Scriptures), a veil is still over their understanding; they read but do not perceive that it points to Christ.

On "which vail is done away in Christ," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the removal: the veil is taken away only "in Christ" — when one comes to Christ (or, the veil is abolished by Christ); he is the key that unlocks the Old Testament's meaning. The verse displays Israel's blindness and the veil removed only in Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Israel's "minds were blinded" (hardened, dulled), not perceiving the law's true end, a judicial and self-incurred blindness (Barnes, Robertson); the veil remaining "to this day" when the Jews read the old covenant, so they read but do not perceive it points to Christ (Barnes); and the veil is taken away only "in Christ" — he unlocks the Old Testament's meaning (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays Israel's blindness. The verse marks the blindness: their minds. The verse displays the veil: in their reading. The verse displays the removal: in Christ. The verse displays the key: Christ. The blindness displays the veil lifted only by coming to Christ. The verse displays the hardening: of Israel. The verse displays the veil: remaining. The verse displays the Christ: the key. Israel's blindness and the veil removed only in Christ display the persisting obscurity of those who reject the gospel: their minds blinded, a veil still lies over the Old Testament as they read it, so they do not see that it points to Christ — and that veil is lifted only in him. The verse displays the necessity of Christ to understand the Scriptures, the veil yet on their heart (v.15).
15:
"But even unto this day, when Moses is read, the vail is upon their heart." The brief verse notes the veil still upon the heart of Israel when Moses is read. The grammatical voices note the persistence. Barnes and Robertson note Paul repeats and intensifies: "even unto this day," when "Moses is read" (the law, in the synagogue), "the vail is upon their heart" — the obscurity is now located not on the text but on their own hearts.

On "upon their heart," the witnesses note the inward blindness. Barnes notes the veil has shifted: it is no longer Moses's face but the Jews' own hearts that are veiled — an inward, spiritual blindness that prevents them from seeing Christ in the Scriptures.

The Reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the point: the veil is now upon the heart, an obstinate spiritual blindness; the fault lies not in the Scripture but in the hardened reader. The verse displays the veil still upon the heart of Israel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul repeats and intensifies — "even unto this day," when "Moses is read" (the law in the synagogue), "the vail is upon their heart," the obscurity located not on the text but on their own hearts (Barnes, Robertson); the veil shifted from Moses's face to the Jews' hearts, an inward spiritual blindness preventing them from seeing Christ in the Scriptures (Barnes). Calvin notes the fault lies not in the Scripture but in the hardened reader. The verse displays the veil on the heart. The verse marks the persistence: unto this day. The verse displays the reading: of Moses. The verse displays the location: upon their heart. The verse displays the blindness: inward. The veil displays the inward spiritual blindness of the reader. The verse displays the veil: on the heart. The verse displays the reading: of the law. The verse displays the obstinacy: of unbelief. The veil still upon the heart of Israel when Moses is read displays the persisting inward blindness of unbelief: the veil now lies not on the text but on the hearts of the readers, an obstinate spiritual obscurity that hides Christ from them in their own Scriptures. The verse displays the heart-blindness of Israel, lifted by turning to the Lord (v.16).
16:
"Nevertheless when it shall turn to the Lord, the vail shall be taken away." The witnesses note the promise that the veil is removed when one turns to the Lord. On "when it shall turn to the Lord," the grammatical voices note the condition. Barnes and Robertson note the gracious promise (echoing Exodus 34:34, where Moses removed the veil when he turned to the Lord): when the heart "turns to the Lord" (to Christ, in conversion), the veil is removed.

On "the vail shall be taken away," the witnesses note the removal. Barnes and Calvin note that turning to Christ takes away the veil of blindness — the convert then sees clearly the meaning of the Scriptures and the glory of the gospel; conversion lifts the obscurity.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the hope: this holds out hope even for blinded Israel — whenever any Jew (or any blinded soul) turns to Christ, the veil is removed and the light breaks in. The verse displays the promise that the veil is removed when one turns to the Lord.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gracious promise (echoing Exodus 34:34, Moses removing the veil when he turned to the Lord) — when the heart "turns to the Lord" (to Christ, in conversion), the veil is removed (Barnes, Robertson); turning to Christ taking away the veil of blindness, so the convert sees clearly the Scriptures and the gospel's glory (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note this holds out hope even for blinded Israel — whenever any turns to Christ, the light breaks in. The verse displays the veil removed by turning to the Lord. The verse marks the condition: turning to the Lord. The verse displays the removal: the veil taken away. The verse displays the sight: clear at last. The verse displays the hope: for the blinded. The promise displays conversion lifting the veil of blindness. The verse displays the turning: to Christ. The verse displays the veil: removed. The verse displays the light: breaking in. The promise that the veil is removed when one turns to the Lord displays the gracious cure for spiritual blindness: when the heart turns to Christ in conversion, the veil is lifted and the glory of the gospel shines clear — a hope held out even to blinded Israel. The verse displays the removal of the veil in conversion, explained by the Lord who is the Spirit (v.17).
17:
"Now the Lord is that Spirit: and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty." The witnesses note the Lord identified with the Spirit, and the liberty of the Spirit — a Christological crux. On "now the Lord is that Spirit," the interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes takes "the Lord" as Christ, who is "the Spirit" — the substance, end, and life of the Old Testament (the "spirit" of v.6), the deity of Christ implied. The Reformed Calvin distinguishes carefully: this speaks not of Christ's essence but his office — Christ is the life-giving spirit of the law (the soul that quickens it), and the "Spirit" in the next clause is the Holy Spirit he confers; he warns against pressing it (as the Arians did) to deny Christ's true deity. Robertson notes "the Lord" may be the Jehovah of Exodus 34:34, the passage quoted; Ellicott notes Christ's essential being is Spirit (John 4:24).

On "where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty," the witnesses note the freedom. Barnes and Cyril note that where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is "liberty" — freedom of access to God, freedom from the veil and bondage of the law, from servile fear, and from the guilt and power of sin (the deity of the Spirit also implied, Cyril).

The witnesses note the connection: turning to the Lord (v.16) brings the Spirit, and the Spirit brings liberty — the open-faced freedom the apostles enjoy. The verse displays the Lord identified with the Spirit and the liberty of the Spirit.

The witnesses agree the new-covenant blessing: where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is "liberty" — freedom of access to God, from the veil and bondage of the law, from servile fear, and from sin (Barnes, Cyril, the deity of the Spirit implied). But "the Lord is that Spirit" divides them: Barnes takes "the Lord" as Christ, who is the Spirit, the substance and life of the Old Testament; Calvin insists it speaks of Christ's office, not essence — Christ the life-giving spirit of the law who confers the Holy Spirit — warning against the Arian misuse; Robertson takes "the Lord" as the Jehovah of the Exodus passage quoted. The verse displays the Lord and the Spirit, and liberty. The verse marks the identification: the Lord is that Spirit. The verse displays the debate: Christ's essence or office, or Jehovah. The verse displays the blessing: liberty. The verse displays the freedom: from bondage. The crux displays the Lord, the Spirit, and the liberty of the gospel. The verse displays the Lord: the Spirit. The verse displays the Spirit: of the Lord. The verse displays the liberty: of access. The Lord identified with the Spirit, and the liberty of the Spirit, display the freedom of the new covenant and a Christological depth: where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty — freedom of access and from bondage — while "the Lord is that Spirit" is read of Christ as the Spirit and substance of the law (Barnes), of his office not essence (Calvin), or of the Jehovah of the quoted passage (Robertson). The verse displays the Spirit's liberty, crowned by the transforming vision of glory (v.18).
18:
"But we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory, even as by the Spirit of the Lord." The witnesses note the transforming vision: beholding the Lord's glory, we are changed into his image. On "we all, with open (unveiled) face beholding... the glory of the Lord," the grammatical voices note the privilege. Barnes and Robertson note that "we all" (all believers, not Moses only) with "open (unveiled) face" — no veil between us and God — behold "as in a glass (mirror)" the glory of the Lord; the gospel reflects Christ's glory to us.

On "are changed into the same image from glory to glory," the witnesses note the transformation. Barnes and Calvin note that beholding this glory, believers "are changed (transformed, metamorphosed) into the same image" — progressively conformed to Christ's likeness, "from glory to glory" (from one degree of glory to another, ever-increasing).

On "even as by the Spirit of the Lord," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Cyril note the agent: this transformation is wrought "by the Spirit of the Lord" — the Holy Spirit effects the progressive sanctification, conforming the believer to Christ. The verse displays the transforming vision of the Lord's glory.

The witnesses agree: "we all" (all believers) with "open (unveiled) face" — no veil between us and God — behold as in a mirror the glory of the Lord reflected in the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); and beholding it, are "changed (transformed) into the same image," progressively conformed to Christ's likeness "from glory to glory," ever-increasing (Barnes, Calvin); this wrought "by the Spirit of the Lord," who effects the progressive sanctification (Calvin, Cyril). The verse displays the transforming vision. The verse marks the beholding: open face, the Lord's glory. The verse displays the change: into his image. The verse displays the increase: glory to glory. The verse displays the agent: the Spirit. The vision displays believers transformed by beholding Christ's glory. The verse displays the unveiled face: beholding. The verse displays the image: Christ's. The verse displays the Spirit: transforming. The transforming vision — beholding the Lord's glory, we are changed into his image — displays the crowning blessing of the new covenant: all believers, with unveiled face, behold Christ's glory in the gospel and are progressively transformed into his likeness, from glory to glory, by the work of the Spirit. The verse displays the Spirit's transforming work and closes the chapter on the surpassing glory of the new-covenant ministry.
